revision.text: 水天とは須弥山の西に住んでいる十二天の一である。
{{d|テスト、定義未満}} 水天とは須弥山の西に住んでいる十二天の一である。

revision.comment: ウィキポータル「仏教」追加、ゾロアスター教との関連追加
revision.text:
{{Buddhism}} '''水天'''（すいてん）とは、[[仏教]]における[[天部]]の一人で、[[須弥山]]の西に住んでいるとされる。[[十二天]]の一である。水の神であり、[[竜]]を支配するとされる。 もともとはインド=イランの古い[[アスラ]]族の[[ヴァルナ]]である。
諸ヴェーダにおいて、重要な位置に置かれ、天空神・司法神（＝契約と正義の神）・水神などの属性をもたされた。ヴァルナは西方では[[アフラマズダ]]となりゾロアスター教の最高神となる。しかし東方ではブラフマン（梵天）に始源神としての地位を奪われており、さらに後には死者を裁くヤマ神に司法神としての地位を奪われ、水神としての属性のみが残った。 仏教に取り入れられた頃は、仏教における十二天の一つ、西方を守護する「水天」となった。
有名な寺院は[[水天宮]]である。ヴァルナ神（水天）は記紀神話でいえば[[天御中主神]]にあたると解釈されたため神仏習合された。水天は安産の神としても有名である。
==関連項目== *[[ヴァルナ]] *[[水天宮]] *[[アーディティヤ神群]] *[[アスラ]] *[[ミスラ]] *[[水天宮 (東京都中央区)]]
[[category:天部|すいてん]] {{buddhism-stub}}

revision.comment: [[水天宮]] 2007-10-14T08:09:39(UTC) 版より水天の説明を転記
revision.text:
{{Buddhism}} '''水天'''（すいてん）とは、[[仏教]]における[[天部]]の一人で、[[須弥山]]の西に住んでいるとされる。[[十二天]]の一である。水の神であり、[[竜]]を支配するとされる。 もともとはインド=イランの古い[[アスラ]]族の[[ヴァルナ]]である。
諸ヴェーダにおいて、重要な位置に置かれ、天空神・司法神（＝契約と正義の神）・水神などの属性をもたされた。ヴァルナは西方では[[アフラマズダ]]となりゾロアスター教の最高神となる。しかし東方ではブラフマン（梵天）に始源神としての地位を奪われており、さらに後には死者を裁くヤマ神に司法神としての地位を奪われ、水神としての属性のみが残った。 仏教に取り入れられた頃は、仏教における十二天の一つ、西方を守護する「水天」となった。
有名な寺院は[[水天宮]]である。ヴァルナ神（水天）は記紀神話でいえば[[天御中主神]]にあたると解釈されたため神仏習合された。水天は安産の神としても有名である。
仏教の神（[[天部]]）である「[[水天]]」の信仰は、[[神仏習合]]時代には「水」の字つながりで「天之水分神・国之水分神」（あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ）と習合していた。ミクマリノカミは本来は子供とは関係なかったと思われるが、「みくまり」の発音が「みこもり」（御子守り）に通じるというので「子育て」の神、子供の守り神として信仰されるようになった。
そもそもの「[[水天]]」は仏教に取り入れられる前は、もともとは古代のイラン・インドの[[ヴァルナ]]という最高神であり、[[バラモン教]]の聖典「[[ヴェーダ]]」の神話にも登場し、[[ゾロアスター教]]ではこの神を[[アフラマズダ]]として称える。神仏分離以後は、[[ヴァルナ]]神は記紀神話でいえば[[天御中主神]]にあたると解釈された。
==関連項目== *[[ヴァルナ]] *[[水天宮]] *[[アーディティヤ神群]] *[[アスラ]] *[[ミスラ]] *[[水天宮 (東京都中央区)]]
[[category:天部|すいてん]] {{buddhism-stub}}

{{Buddhism}} '''水天'''（すいてん）とは、[[仏教]]における[[天部]]の一人で、[[須弥山]]の西に住んでいるとされる。[[十二天]]の一である。水の神であり、[[竜]]を支配するとされる。もともとはインド=イランの古い[[アスラ]]族の[[ヴァルナ]]である。
諸ヴェーダにおいて、重要な位置に置かれ、天空神・司法神（＝契約と正義の神）・水神などの属性をもたされた。ヴァルナは西方では[[アフラマズダ]]となりゾロアスター教の最高神となる。しかし東方ではブラフマン（梵天）に始源神としての地位を奪われており、さらに後には死者を裁くヤマ神に司法神としての地位を奪われ、水神としての属性のみが残った。仏教に取り入れられた頃は、仏教における十二天の一つ、西方を守護する「水天」となった。
日本においては、[[神仏習合]]時代に「水」の字つながりで「[[水分神|天之水分神・国之水分神]]」（あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ）と同一視された。水分神は本来は子供とは関係なかったと思われるが、「みくまり」の発音が「みこもり」（御子守り）に通じるというので、水天は安産・子育ての神・子供の守り神として信仰されるようになった。[[神仏分離]]の際、ヴァルナ神（水天）は[[天御中主神]]に当たると解釈され、水天を祀る[[水天宮]]の祭神は天御中主神に変えられた。
==関連項目== *[[ヴァルナ]] *[[水天宮]] *[[アーディティヤ神群]] *[[アスラ]] *[[ミスラ]]
[[category:天部|すいてん]] {{buddhism-stub}}

[[Image:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|left|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127年）]] '''水天'''（すいてん）とは、[[仏教]]における[[天部]]の一人で、[[須弥山]]の西に住んでいるとされる。[[十二天]]の一である。
==神格== 水の神であり、[[竜]]を支配するとされる。もともとはインド=イランの古い[[アスラ]]族の[[ヴァルナ (神)|ヴァルナ]]である。 諸ヴェーダにおいて、ヴァルナは重要な位置に置かれ、天空神・司法神（＝契約と正義の神）・水神などの属性をもたされた。ヴァルナは西方では[[アフラマズダ]]となりゾロアスター教の最高神となる。
しかし東方ではブラフマン（梵天）に始源神としての地位を奪われており、さらに後には死者を裁くヤマ神に司法神としての地位を奪われ、水神としての属性のみが残った。仏教に取り入れられた頃は、仏教における十二天の一つ、西方を守護する「水天」となった。
==日本の水天== [[日本]]においては、[[神仏習合]]時代に「水」の字つながりで「[[水分神|天之水分神・国之水分神]]」（あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ）と習合した。水分神は本来は子供とは関係なかったと思われるが、「みくまり」の発音が「みこもり」（御子守り）に通じるというので、水天は安産・子育ての神・子供の守り神として信仰されるようになった。
[[神仏分離]]の際、ヴァルナ神のもともとの神格が最高神・始源神であることから、[[記紀神話]]における始源神・[[天御中主神]]に当たると解釈され、水天を祀る[[水天宮]]の祭神は天御中主神に変えられた。また、[[安徳天皇]]が在位のまま入水したことから水天皇と呼ばれ、水天と同一視された。
==関連項目== *[[仏の一覧]] *[[アーディティヤ神群]] *[[ミスラ]]
{{Buddhism2}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:すいてん}} [[Category:天部]] [[category:十二天]] [[Category:水神]]

[[Image:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|left|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127年）]] '''水天'''（すいてん）とは、[[仏教]]における[[天部]]の一人で、[[須弥山]]の西に住んでいるとされる。[[十二天]]の一である。

竜から転じて、地方や寺院によっては[[蛇]]がその象徴たる動物と扱われることがある。

revision.comment: /* 日本の水天 */ 国宝仏画追記
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2016年10月15日 (土) 13:46 (UTC)}} [[ファイル:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127年）]] '''水天'''（すいてん）とは、[[仏教]]における[[天部]]の一人で、[[須弥山]]の西に住んでいるとされる。[[十二天]]の一である。
== 神格 == 水の神であり、[[竜]]を支配するとされる。もともとはインド=イランの古い[[アスラ]]族の[[ヴァルナ (神)|ヴァルナ]]である。 諸ヴェーダにおいて、ヴァルナは重要な位置に置かれ、天空神・司法神（＝契約と正義の神）・水神などの属性をもたされた。ヴァルナは西方では[[アフラ・マズダー]]となり[[ゾロアスター教]]の最高神となる。
しかし東方では[[ブラフマー|ブラフマン]]（梵天）に始源神としての地位を奪われており、さらに後には死者を裁く[[閻魔|ヤマ]]神に司法神としての地位を奪われ、水神としての属性のみが残った。仏教に取り入れられた頃は、仏教における十二天の一つ、西方を守護する「水天」となった。
== 真言 == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ
== 日本の水天 == [[日本]]においては、[[神仏習合]]時代に「水」の字つながりで「[[水分神|天之水分神・国之水分神]]」（あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ）と習合した。水分神は本来は子供とは関係なかったと思われるが、「みくまり」の発音が「みこもり」（御子守り）に通じるというので、水天は安産・子育ての神・子供の守り神として信仰されるようになった。
[[神仏分離]]の際、ヴァルナ神のもともとの神格が最高神・始源神であることから、[[記紀神話]]における始源神・[[天御中主神]]に当たると解釈され、水天を祀る[[水天宮]]の祭神は天御中主神に変えられた。また、[[安徳天皇]]が在位のまま入水したことから水天皇と呼ばれ、水天と同一視された。
竜から転じて、地方や寺院によっては[[蛇]]がその象徴たる動物と扱われることがある。
[[国宝]]として、[[京都国立博物館]]に[[仏画]]（絹本著色144.3 x 126.5cm）の水天像が所蔵されており、同作品は[[平安時代]]以来、[[宮中・京中の式内社一覧|宮中]]の真言院で毎年正月に行なわれた[[修法]]に用いるため、[[1127年]]（[[大治 (日本)|大治]]2年）に制作された[[十二天]]画像のうちの1つである<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|title=水天像（すいてんぞう）（十二天のうち）|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=京都国立博物館}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{節スタブ|1=脚注形式での参照ページ番号の明記|date=2016年10月15日 (土) 13:46 (UTC)}}

{{出典の明記|date=2016年10月15日 (土) 13:46 (UTC)}} [[ファイル:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127年）]] '''水天'''（すいてん）とは、[[仏教]]における[[天部]]の一人で、[[須弥山]]の西に住んでいるとされる。[[十二天]]の一である。
== 神格 == 水の神であり、[[竜]]を支配するとされる。もともとはインド=イランの古い[[アスラ]]族の[[ヴァルナ (神)|ヴァルナ]]である。 諸ヴェーダにおいて、ヴァルナは重要な位置に置かれ、天空神・司法神（＝契約と正義の神）・水神などの属性をもたされた。ヴァルナは西方では[[アフラ・マズダー]]となり[[ゾロアスター教]]の最高神となる。
しかし東方では[[ブラフマー|ブラフマン]]（梵天）に始源神としての地位を奪われており、さらに後には死者を裁く[[閻魔|ヤマ]]神に司法神としての地位を奪われ、水神としての属性のみが残った。仏教に取り入れられた頃は、仏教における十二天の一つ、西方を守護する「水天」となった。
十二天は、八方位と上下二方、それに日月を加えた方位を守護する神で、[[インド]]の[[ヴェーダ]]神話に登場する古い[[神格]]である<ref name=":0" />。
== 真言 == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ
== 日本の水天 == [[日本]]においては、[[神仏習合]]時代に「水」の字つながりで「[[水分神|天之水分神・国之水分神]]」（あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ）と習合した。水分神は本来は子供とは関係なかったと思われるが、「みくまり」の発音が「みこもり」（御子守り）に通じるというので、水天は安産・子育ての神・子供の守り神として信仰されるようになった。
[[神仏分離]]の際、ヴァルナ神のもともとの神格が最高神・始源神であることから、[[記紀神話]]における始源神・[[天御中主神]]に当たると解釈され、水天を祀る[[水天宮]]の祭神は天御中主神に変えられた。また、[[安徳天皇]]が在位のまま入水したことから水天皇と呼ばれ、水天と同一視された。
竜から転じて、地方や寺院によっては[[蛇]]がその象徴たる動物と扱われることがある。
[[国宝]]として、[[京都国立博物館]]に[[仏画]]（絹本著色144.3 x 126.5cm）の水天像が所蔵されており、同作品は[[平安時代]]以来、[[宮中・京中の式内社一覧|宮中]]の真言院で毎年正月に行なわれた[[修法]]に用いるため、[[1127年]]（[[大治 (日本)|大治]]2年）に制作された十二天画像のうちの1つである<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|title=水天像（すいてんぞう）（十二天のうち）|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=京都国立博物館}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{節スタブ|1=脚注形式での参照ページ番号の明記|date=2016年10月15日 (土) 13:46 (UTC)}}
== 参考文献 == <!--この節には、記事本文の編集時に実際に参考にした書籍等のみを記載して下さい。 書籍の宣伝目的の掲載はおやめ下さい。--> {{節スタブ|date=2016年10月15日 (土) 13:46 (UTC)}}
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Suiten}} * [[仏の一覧]] * [[アーディティヤ神群]] * [[ミスラ]] * [[水神]]
{{Buddhism2}} {{デフォルトソート:すいてん}} [[Category:天部]] [[category:十二天]] [[Category:水神]] [[Category:水天宮|*すいてん]] [[Category:神仏習合]] {{buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}}

オン バロダヤ ソワカ
== 日本の水天 == [[日本]]においては、[[神仏習合]]時代に「水」の字つながりで「[[水分神|天之水分神・国之水分神]]」（あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ）と習合した。水分神は本来は子供とは関係なかったと思われるが、「みくまり」の発音が「みこもり」（御子守り）に通じるというので、水天は安産・子育ての神・子供の守り神として信仰されるようになった。
{{要出典範囲|date=2019年12月|[[神仏分離]]の際、ヴァルナ神のもともとの神格が最高神・始源神であることから、[[日本神話|記紀神話]]における始源神・[[天御中主神]]に当たると解釈され、水天を祀る[[水天宮]]の祭神は天御中主神に変えられた。また、[[安徳天皇]]が在位のまま入水したことから水天皇と呼ばれ、水天と同一視された。}}
竜から転じて、地方や寺院によっては[[蛇]]がその象徴たる動物と扱われることがある。
[[国宝]]として、[[京都国立博物館]]に[[仏画]]（絹本著色144.3 x 126.5cm）の水天像が所蔵されており、同作品は[[平安時代]]以来、[[宮中・京中の式内社一覧|宮中]]の真言院で毎年正月に行なわれた[[修法]]に用いるため、[[1127年]]（[[大治 (日本)|大治]]2年）に制作された[[十二天]]画像のうちの1つである<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|title=水天像（すいてんぞう）（十二天のうち）|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=京都国立博物館}}</ref>。
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|水天像。隼人塚資料館所蔵]]
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{節スタブ|1=脚注形式での参照ページ番号の明記|date=2016年10月15日 (土) 13:46 (UTC)}}
== 参考文献 == <!--この節には、記事本文の編集時に実際に参考にした書籍等のみを記載して下さい。 書籍の宣伝目的の掲載はおやめ下さい。--> {{節スタブ|date=2016年10月15日 (土) 13:46 (UTC)}}
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Suiten}} * [[仏の一覧]] * [[アーディティヤ神群]] * [[ミスラ]] * [[水神]]
{{Buddhism2}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{shinto-stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:すいてん}} [[Category:天部]] [[Category:十二天]] [[Category:水神]] [[Category:水天宮|+すいてん]] [[Category:神仏習合]]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2016年10月15日 (土) 13:46 (UTC)}} [[ファイル:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127年）]] '''水天'''（すいてん）とは、[[仏教]]における[[天部]]の一人で、[[須弥山]]の西に住んでいるとされる。[[十二天]]の一である。
== 神格 == 水の神であり、[[竜]]を支配するとされる。もともとはインド=イランの古い[[アスラ]]族の[[ヴァルナ (神)|ヴァルナ]]である。 諸ヴェーダにおいて、ヴァルナは重要な位置に置かれ、天空神・司法神（＝契約と正義の神）・水神などの属性をもたされた。ヴァルナは西方では[[アフラ・マズダー]]となり[[ゾロアスター教]]の最高神となる。
しかし東方では[[ブラフマー]]（梵天）に始源神としての地位を奪われており、さらに後には死者を裁く[[閻魔|ヤマ]]神に司法神としての地位を奪われ、水神としての属性のみが残った。仏教に取り入れられた頃は、仏教における十二天の一つ、西方を守護する「水天」となった。
== 真言 == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>[[悟東あすか]]『神さま仏さまがこっそり教えてくれたこと』2018年、ダイヤモンド社、202ページ</ref>
== 日本の水天 == [[日本]]においては、[[神仏習合]]時代に「水」の字つながりで「[[水分神|天之水分神・国之水分神]]」（あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ）と習合した。水分神は本来は子供とは関係なかったと思われるが、「みくまり」の発音が「みこもり」（御子守り）に通じるというので、水天は安産・子育ての神・子供の守り神として信仰されるようになった。
{{要出典範囲|date=2019年12月|[[神仏分離]]の際、ヴァルナ神のもともとの神格が最高神・始源神であることから、[[日本神話|記紀神話]]における始源神・[[天御中主神]]に当たると解釈され、水天を祀る[[水天宮]]の祭神は天御中主神に変えられた。また、[[安徳天皇]]が在位のまま入水したことから水天皇と呼ばれ、水天と同一視された。}}
竜から転じて、地方や寺院によっては[[蛇]]がその象徴たる動物と扱われることがある。
[[国宝]]として、[[京都国立博物館]]に[[仏画]]（絹本著色144.3 x 126.5cm）の水天像が所蔵されており、同作品は[[平安時代]]以来、[[宮中・京中の式内社一覧|宮中]]の真言院で毎年正月に行なわれた[[修法]]に用いるため、[[1127年]]（[[大治 (日本)|大治]]2年）に制作された[[十二天]]画像のうちの1つである<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|title=水天像（すいてんぞう）（十二天のうち）|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=京都国立博物館}}</ref>。
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|水天像。隼人塚資料館所蔵]]
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{節スタブ|1=脚注形式での参照ページ番号の明記|date=2016年10月15日 (土) 13:46 (UTC)}}
== 参考文献 == <!--この節には、記事本文の編集時に実際に参考にした書籍等のみを記載して下さい。 書籍の宣伝目的の掲載はおやめ下さい。--> {{節スタブ|date=2016年10月15日 (土) 13:46 (UTC)}}

revision.text:
{{Source citation|date=October 15, 2016 (Sat) 13:46 (UTC)}} [[File:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127th year ）]]
'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|ja|十二天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelve Devas|qq=}}.
== Divine Status == He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].
In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].
However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|qq=}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|qq=}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the {{ill|Snake|ja|蛇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Snake|qq=}} is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|ja|十二天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelve Devas|qq=}} produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]
== Footnotes ==
{{reflist}} {{Section stub|1=Specification of reference page numbers in footnote format|date=October 15, 2016 (Sat) 13:46 (UTC)}}
== References ==
{{Section stub|date=October 15, 2016 (Sat) 13:46 (UTC)}}

<!-- In this section, please list only the books and other works that were actually referenced during the editing of the article. Please refrain from including books for promotional purposes. --> [[Category:十二天]] [[Category:天部]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:Water deities]]

{{Source citation|date=October 15, 2016 (Sat) 13:46 (UTC)}} [[File:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127th year ）]]
'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|ja|十二天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelve Devas|qq=}}.
== Divine Status == He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].
In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].
However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|qq=}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|qq=}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the {{ill|Snake|ja|蛇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Snake|qq=}} is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|ja|十二天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelve Devas|qq=}} produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]
== Footnotes ==

This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|ja|十二天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Twelve Devas|qq=}} produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

{{Source citation|date=October 15, 2016 (Sat) 13:46 (UTC)}} [[File:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127th year ）]]
'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]].
== Divine Status == He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].
In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].
However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|qq=}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the {{ill|Snake|ja|蛇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Snake|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヘビ}} is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|qq=}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]].
== Divine Status == He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].
In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].
However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|qq=}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>

Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]].

== Divine Status ==
He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].

In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].

However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.

== Mantra ==
oṃ varuṇāya svāhā

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|qq=|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>

In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.

{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}

By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.

As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.

{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}

By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.

As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]].

== Divine Status ==
He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].

In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].

However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.

== Mantra ==
oṃ varuṇāya svāhā

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|qq=|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>

== Suiten in Japan ==
In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.

{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}

By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.

As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.

{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}

By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.

As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suiten]][[ja:水天]][[ko:수천]][[zh:水天]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11548435]] Wikidata name: Suiten [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Source citation|date=October 15, 2016 (Sat) 13:46 (UTC)}} [[File:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127th year ）]]
'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]].
== Divine Status == He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].
In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].
However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>

Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>

Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suiten]][[ja:水天]][[ko:수천]][[zh:水天]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11548435]] Wikidata name: Suiten [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Source citation|date=October 15, 2016 (Sat) 13:46 (UTC)}} [[File:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127th year ）]]
'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]].
== Divine Status == He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].
In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].
However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suiten]][[ja:水天]][[ko:수천]][[zh:水天]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11548435]] Wikidata name: Suiten [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Source citation|date=October 15, 2016 (Sat) 13:46 (UTC)}} [[File:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127th year ）]]
'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]].
== Divine Status == He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].
In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].
However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]].
== Divine Status == He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].
In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].
However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]’s [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Celestial Beings]], who is said to live to the west of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Sumeru]]. He is one of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]].
== Divine Status == He is the god of water and is said to control [[:en:Dragon|Dragon]]. Originally, he was the [[:en:Asura|Asura]] of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]].
In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became [[:en:Ahura Mazda|Ahura Mazda]] and the supreme god of [[:en:Zoroastrianism|Zoroastrianism]].
However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, [[:en:Yama|Yama]], leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.
== Mantra == oṃ varuṇāya svāhā
オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>

Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>
== Suiten in Japan == In Japan, during the period of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu Shūgō]], by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.
{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]], since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the [[:en:Japanese mythology|Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths]], and the enshrined deity of [[:en:Suitengū|Suiten Shrine]] was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]] entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}
By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the [[:en:Snake|Snake]] is regarded as its symbolic animal.
As a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]], a [[:en:Buddhist painting|Buddhist Painting]] (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and it is one of the images of the [[:en:Twelve Devas|Twelve Devas]] produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of [[:en:Daiji (era)|Daiji]]).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

[[File:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127th year ）]]

'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}’s {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|WD=Q4089509|lt=Celestial Beings}}, who is said to live to the west of {{ill|Mount Meru|en|Mount Meru|WD=Q652550|lt=Mount Sumeru}}. He is one of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|en|Twelve Devas|WD=Q9503981|lt=Twelve Devas}}.

== Divine Status ==
He is the god of water and is said to control {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon}}. Originally, he was the {{ill|Asura|en|Asura|WD=Q624083|lt=Asura}} of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as {{ill|Varuna|en|Varuna|WD=Q1001037|lt=Varuna}}.

In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became {{ill|Ahura Mazda|en|Ahura Mazda|WD=Q179575|lt=Ahura Mazda}} and the supreme god of {{ill|Zoroastrianism|en|Zoroastrianism|WD=Q9601|lt=Zoroastrianism}}.

However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by {{ill|Brahma|en|Brahma|WD=Q11389|lt=Brahma}}, and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama}}, leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.

== Mantra ==
oṃ varuṇāya svāhā

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>

== Suiten in Japan ==
In Japan, during the period of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō}}, by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.

{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism}}, since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths}}, and the enshrined deity of {{ill|Suitengū|en|Suitengū|WD=Q125319456|lt=Suiten Shrine}} was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|WD=Q316651|lt=Emperor Antoku}} entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}

By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}} is regarded as its symbolic animal.

As a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}}, a {{ill|Buddhist painting|en|Buddhist painting|WD=Q11129163|lt=Buddhist Painting}} (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum}}. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, and it is one of the images of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|en|Twelve Devas|WD=Q9503981|lt=Twelve Devas}} produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of {{ill|Daiji (era)|en|Daiji (era)|WD=Q1037046|lt=Daiji}}).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, and it is one of the images of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|en|Twelve Devas|WD=Q9503981|lt=Twelve Devas}} produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of {{ill|Daiji (era)|en|Daiji (era)|WD=Q1037046|lt=Daiji}}).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]
