<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suiten]][[ja:水天]][[ko:수천]][[zh:水天]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suiten]][[ja:水天]][[ko:수천]][[zh:水天]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suiten]][[ja:水天]][[ko:수천]][[zh:水天]]

{{Source citation|date=October 15, 2016 (Sat) 13:46 (UTC)}}
[[File:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127th year ）]]

'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}’s {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|WD=Q4089509|lt=Celestial Beings}}, who is said to live to the west of {{ill|Mount Meru|en|Mount Meru|WD=Q652550|lt=Mount Sumeru}}. He is one of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|en|Twelve Devas|WD=Q9503981|lt=Twelve Devas}}.

== Divine Status ==
He is the god of water and is said to control {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon}}. Originally, he was the {{ill|Asura|en|Asura|WD=Q624083|lt=Asura}} of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as {{ill|Varuna|en|Varuna|WD=Q1001037|lt=Varuna}}.

In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became {{ill|Ahura Mazda|en|Ahura Mazda|WD=Q179575|lt=Ahura Mazda}} and the supreme god of {{ill|Zoroastrianism|en|Zoroastrianism|WD=Q9601|lt=Zoroastrianism}}.

However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by {{ill|Brahma|en|Brahma|WD=Q11389|lt=Brahma}}, and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama}}, leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.

== Mantra ==
oṃ varuṇāya svāhā

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>

== Suiten in Japan ==
In Japan, during the period of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō}}, by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.

{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism}}, since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths}}, and the enshrined deity of {{ill|Suitengū|en|Suitengū|WD=Q125319456|lt=Suiten Shrine}} was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|WD=Q316651|lt=Emperor Antoku}} entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}

By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake}} is regarded as its symbolic animal.

As a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}}, a {{ill|Buddhist painting|en|Buddhist painting|WD=Q11129163|lt=Buddhist Painting}} (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum}}. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, and it is one of the images of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|en|Twelve Devas|WD=Q9503981|lt=Twelve Devas}} produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of {{ill|Daiji (era)|en|Daiji (era)|WD=Q1037046|lt=Daiji}}).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suiten]][[ja:水天]][[ko:수천]][[zh:水天]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suiten]][[ja:水天]][[ko:수천]][[zh:水天]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Suiten]][[ja:水天]][[ko:수천]][[zh:水天]]

{{Source citation|date=October 15, 2016 (Sat) 13:46 (UTC)}}
[[File:Sui Ten Varna TOJI12TEN.JPG|right|thumb|200px|水天（部分・1127th year ）]]

'''Suiten''' (水天) is one of the {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}’s {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|WD=Q4089509|lt=Celestial Beings|1=Deva (Buddhism)}}, who is said to live to the west of {{ill|Mount Meru|en|Mount Meru|WD=Q652550|lt=Mount Sumeru|1=Mount Meru}}. He is one of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|en|Twelve Devas|WD=Q9503981|lt=Twelve Devas|1=Jūniten}}.

== Divine Status ==
He is the god of water and is said to control {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon|1=Dragon}}. Originally, he was the {{ill|Asura|en|Asura|WD=Q624083|lt=Asura|1=Asura}} of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as {{ill|Varuna|en|Varuna|WD=Q1001037|lt=Varuna|1=Varuna}}.

In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became {{ill|Ahura Mazda|en|Ahura Mazda|WD=Q179575|lt=Ahura Mazda|1=Ahura Mazda}} and the supreme god of {{ill|Zoroastrianism|en|Zoroastrianism|WD=Q9601|lt=Zoroastrianism|1=Zoroastrianism}}.

However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by {{ill|Brahma|en|Brahma|WD=Q11389|lt=Brahma|1=Brahma}}, and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama|1=Yama}}, leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.

== Mantra ==
oṃ varuṇāya svāhā

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>

== Suiten in Japan ==
In Japan, during the period of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.

{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths|1=Japanese mythology}}, and the enshrined deity of {{ill|Suitengū|en|Suitengū|WD=Q125319456|lt=Suiten Shrine|1=Suitengū}} was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|WD=Q316651|lt=Emperor Antoku|1=Emperor Antoku}} entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}

By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake|1=Snake}} is regarded as its symbolic animal.

As a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}, a {{ill|Buddhist painting|en|Buddhist painting|WD=Q11129163|lt=Buddhist Painting|1=Buddhist painting}} (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}}. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, and it is one of the images of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|en|Twelve Devas|WD=Q9503981|lt=Twelve Devas|1=Jūniten}} produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of {{ill|Daiji (era)|en|Daiji (era)|WD=Q1037046|lt=Daiji|1=Daiji (era)}}).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>

[[File:Suiten Statue (Hayato).jpg|thumb|Water Deity Statue. Hayato-zuka Museum collection]]

He is one of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|en|Twelve Devas|WD=Q9503981|lt=Twelve Devas|1=Jūniten}}.

== Divine Status ==
He is the god of water and is said to control {{ill|Dragon|en|Dragon|WD=Q7559|lt=Dragon|1=Dragon}}. Originally, he was the {{ill|Asura|en|Asura|WD=Q624083|lt=Asura|1=Asura}} of the ancient Indo-Iranian people, known as {{ill|Varuna|en|Varuna|WD=Q1001037|lt=Varuna|1=Varuna}}.

In the Vedas, Varuna held an important position and was endowed with the attributes of a sky god, a god of justice (that is, the deity of contracts and righteousness), and a water god. In the western regions, Varuna became {{ill|Ahura Mazda|en|Ahura Mazda|WD=Q179575|lt=Ahura Mazda|1=Ahura Mazda}} and the supreme god of {{ill|Zoroastrianism|en|Zoroastrianism|WD=Q9601|lt=Zoroastrianism|1=Zoroastrianism}}.

However, in the eastern regions, his status as the primordial god was usurped by {{ill|Brahma|en|Brahma|WD=Q11389|lt=Brahma|1=Brahma}}, and later his role as the god of justice was taken over by the god of the dead, {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|WD=Q45097|lt=Yama|1=Yama}}, leaving him with only his attribute as a water god. When incorporated into Buddhism, he became known as “Suiten,” one of the Twelve Devas guarding the west.

== Mantra ==
oṃ varuṇāya svāhā

オン バロダヤ ソワカ<ref>{{ill|Gotō Asuka|ja|悟東あすか|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gotō Asuka|1=Asuka Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492470}} "What the Gods and Buddhas Secretly Taught Me", 2018, Diamond Inc., p.202</ref>

== Suiten in Japan ==
In Japan, during the period of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu Shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, by the association with the character for “water,” Suiten became syncretized with {{ill|Mizubunshin|ja|水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|lt=Heavenly and National Water Deity|lt_ja=天之水分神・国之水分神|1=Mikumari|2=ja|3=水分神|4=zh|5=水分神|6=sv|7=Mikumari-no-Kami|8=fr|9=Mikumari|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizubunshin|qq=}} (あめのみくまりのかみ・くにのみくまりのかみ). Although Mizubunshin originally had no relation to children, the pronunciation “mikumari” came to be associated with “mikomori” (childcare), and thus Suiten came to be revered as a deity of safe childbirth, child-rearing, and as a protector of children.

{{Citation needed|date=December 2019|During the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, since the original divine status of Varuna was that of the supreme and primordial god, it was interpreted as equivalent to the primordial god {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天御中主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天之御中主神}} in the {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Kojiki-Kuniyoshi Myths|1=Japanese mythology}}, and the enshrined deity of {{ill|Suitengū|en|Suitengū|WD=Q125319456|lt=Suiten Shrine|1=Suitengū}} was changed to Ame-no-Minakanushi. Furthermore, since {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|WD=Q316651|lt=Emperor Antoku|1=Emperor Antoku}} entered the water while still reigning, he was called the Water Emperor and came to be identified with Suiten.}}

By extension from the dragon, in some regions and temples the {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|WD=Q2102|lt=Snake|1=Snake}} is regarded as its symbolic animal.

As a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}, a {{ill|Buddhist painting|en|Buddhist painting|WD=Q11129163|lt=Buddhist Painting|1=Buddhist painting}} (painted on silk, 144.3 x 126.5 cm) depicting the water deity is housed at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}}. This work has been used in the annual New Year’s ritual at the Imperial Court’s Shingon-in since the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, and it is one of the images of the {{ill|Twelve Devas|en|Twelve Devas|WD=Q9503981|lt=Twelve Devas|1=Jūniten}} produced in 1127 (the 2nd year of {{ill|Daiji (era)|en|Daiji (era)|WD=Q1037046|lt=Daiji|1=Daiji (era)}}).<ref>{{Cite web|title=Water Deity Statue (Suiten-zō) (Among the Twelve Devas)|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/syuzou/meihin/butsuga/item05_07.html|accessdate=2019-02-08|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|language=ja}}</ref>
